title: An Alternative History for America at 250
gist: Marking America's 250th anniversary, Adekeye Adebajo argues an honest reckoning must weigh the US's role building the post-1945 global order against its racist and imperialist legacy, which keeps the Global South skeptical of America living up to its own ideals.

insights.claim: Properly commemorating the United States' 250th anniversary requires an honest appraisal of both its founding principles and its racist and imperialist legacy, not a celebration of one side alone.
insights.speaker: Adekeye Adebajo
insights.so_what: Sets up a framework for reading US soft-power and legitimacy narratives skeptically, relevant to assessing US standing in Global South diplomacy and trade relationships.
insights.quote: Commemorating the 250th anniversary of the United States requires an honest appraisal of not only its founding principles but also its racist and imperialist legacy.
insights.locator: An Alternative History for America at 250

insights.claim: By the turn of the 20th century the United States had become a leading industrial power, and it achieved global primacy after the two world wars.
insights.speaker: Adekeye Adebajo
insights.so_what: Historical baseline for the trajectory of US economic and geopolitical dominance that later chapters of the piece juxtapose against its imperialist record.
insights.quote: By the turn of the 20th century, it was a leading industrial power and achieved global primacy after the two world wars.

insights.claim: The United States built the core post-1945 global governance architecture — the United Nations, the World Bank, the IMF, and GATT (which became the WTO) — that helped prevent a third world war and advanced decolonization across Africa and Asia.
insights.speaker: Adekeye Adebajo
insights.so_what: Reminds investors of the institutional foundations (UN/World Bank/IMF/WTO) underpinning the current global economic order and whose durability is tied to continued US legitimacy.
insights.quote: It built the post-1945global governance system—the United Nations, the World Bank, the International Monetary Fund, and the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (which became the World Trade Organization)—that helped prevent a third world war and advanced decolonization across Africa and Asia.

insights.claim: A clear-eyed assessment of America's racist and imperialist history explains why many people in the Global South remain cynical about the country's ability to live up to its own founding ideals.
insights.speaker: Adekeye Adebajo
insights.so_what: Useful context for gauging Global South receptivity to US-led initiatives versus alternative powers (e.g., China) courting the same countries.
insights.quote: A clear-eyed assessment shows why many people in the Global South remain cynical about the country’s ability to live up to its ideals.
